text: The assistant introduces a metallic malleable retractor into the vagina, aiding in the mobilization of the uterus and exposing the anterior vaginal wall.
The surgeon gently dissects the vesico-vaginal septum using a swab, halting the dissection just above the bladder neck.
A shaped biological mesh is positioned along the prepared vaginal wall and secured at its deepest point.
The surgeon places four non-absorbable sutures on the prepared vaginal wall, securing the tissue in place.
The surgeon fixes the last point of the biological mesh close to the anterior cervix, completing the mesh placement on the prepared vaginal wall.

After suturing the mesh, two polypropylene strings are passed through the side wall of the abdomen in the preperitoneal space and are externalized three centimeters above the anterior and superior iliac spine.
The surgeon fixes the two ends of the polypropylene strings to the apical portion of the biological mesh using two non-absorbable stitches, securing the mesh in place.
The surgeon removes the vaginal retractor and sutures the peritoneum, completing the closure of the surgical site.
At the end of the procedure, the polypropylene tails are visible protruding from the skin, affixed to the abdominal external oblique muscle.
The surgeon begins the second step of the surgery, which involves suspending the posterior compartment.
The video shows the reflection of the peritoneum, known as the pouch of Douglas, descending completely to the level of the pelvic floor.
The surgeon incises the Denonvilliers fascia, opening the rectovaginal septum to expose underlying structures.
The surgical field shows no rectal mobilization or lateral dissection being performed, with the rectum remaining in its current position.
The surgeon passes a stitch onto the sacral promontory, securing it in place as part of the rectal prolapse repair procedure.
A strip of biological mesh is inserted and sutured to the ventral aspect of the distal rectum, then further secured to the lateral seromuscular borders of the proximal rectum.
The surgeon fixes the proximal part of the biological mesh to the sacral promontory, ensuring there is no traction applied to the rectum.
